skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Guides agents through safe Git worktree setup for isolated development, including directory selection, gitignore checks, setup commands, and baseline tests.
skill.summary: Create isolated Git worktrees for parallel development without disturbing the current working tree.
skill.author: CodingCossack

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell and filesystem patterns in SKILL.md. Most are false positives because they are instructional Git worktree commands, but the setup section can run project-controlled package manager, build, and test commands. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential theft, network exfiltration, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Project-Controlled Command Execution During Setup
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run package manager, build, and test commands after creating a worktree. This is legitimate for development setup, but commands such as npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, npm test, pytest, and go test can execute project-controlled code or download dependencies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically intended to execute local project setup and tests. The use is legitimate, but the security impact is real when the repository is untrusted.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Git Worktree Shell Commands
low_findings.description: The static command-execution findings mostly identify documented shell examples for ls, grep, basename, git worktree, cd, and status reporting. These commands support the stated worktree workflow and do not show command injection, obfuscation, or exfiltration intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands are visible in Markdown examples and align with the skill purpose. No evidence found that arguments are used for hidden behavior beyond worktree creation.

low_findings.title: Benign Hidden Directory References
low_findings.description: The static filesystem findings identify .worktrees, .gitignore, CLAUDE.md, and ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees as storage or configuration locations. These references do not read secrets or hidden credentials, but they do guide agents to create or edit local project files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are clearly related to worktree storage and gitignore safety checks. There is no evidence of sensitive file harvesting or unauthorized home directory traversal.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding appears to match ordinary prose in the skill metadata and integration section. No cryptographic function, hashing API, encryption routine, or credential handling code is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows descriptive text, not cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Dependency, Build, And Test Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup workflow recommends automatically running package installation, build, and test commands. This pattern can execute repository-defined scripts and should be used only in trusted repositories or after user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly listed and include common tools that may run lifecycle hooks or local test code. The risk depends on repository trust, not on malicious intent in the skill itself.

content.value_statement: Parallel feature work can disrupt an active working tree and make changes hard to isolate. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable Git worktree workflow with directory checks, setup steps, and baseline validation.
actual_capabilities: Selects an existing .worktrees or worktrees directory when project conventions already exist.
Checks CLAUDE.md for a documented worktree directory preference before asking the user.
Verifies project-local worktree directories are ignored by .gitignore before use.
Creates a new Git worktree and branch for isolated implementation work.
Runs detected setup and test commands for common Node.js, Rust, Python, and Go projects.
Reports the worktree location and baseline test status before implementation begins.
limitations: Requires Git and a repository that supports git worktree commands.
May run dependency, build, or test commands that execute project-controlled scripts.
Does not resolve failing baseline tests without user direction.
Does not replace project-specific onboarding or environment setup instructions.

use_cases.title: Isolated Feature Branch Setup
use_cases.description: Create a separate worktree before starting implementation so the current working tree remains unchanged.

use_cases.title: Parallel Agent Development
use_cases.description: Give each agent or task a separate branch and directory to reduce conflicts during concurrent work.

use_cases.title: Safe Baseline Validation
use_cases.description: Run setup and tests in a fresh worktree before making changes, so existing failures are visible early.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Worktree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for my next feature. Follow the repository directory preference and report the final location.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Clean Feature Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Git worktree for feature work named after the task. Verify .gitignore if you use a project-local directory, then create the branch and run baseline tests.

prompt_templates.title: Use Existing Project Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree using this repository's existing conventions. Check .worktrees, worktrees, and CLAUDE.md before asking me for a directory decision.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Parallel Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare separate worktrees for two independent implementation tasks. Use clear branch names, verify each baseline, and summarize which directory belongs to each task.

output_examples.input: Create a worktree for an authentication refactor.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects the existing project-local worktree directory, verifies it is ignored, creates a new branch, installs project dependencies, runs tests, and reports the ready workspace.

output_examples.input: Set up isolated work for a bug fix in a repository with no worktree directory.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks project guidance, asks where to place worktrees, creates the selected location, validates ignore rules when needed, and reports any baseline test failures.

output_examples.input: Start two independent tasks without touching my current changes.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates separate worktrees and branches, keeps the current working tree unchanged, and summarizes each workspace with its setup and test status.

best_practices: Use project-local worktrees only after confirming the directory is listed in .gitignore.
Run baseline tests before implementation so existing failures are separated from new changes.
Use clear branch and directory names that match the task being implemented.
anti_patterns: Creating worktrees in an arbitrary location when the repository already has a convention.
Running setup commands in an untrusted repository without reviewing the project scripts first.
Continuing implementation after failing baseline tests without user approval.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps agents create isolated Git workspaces so feature work does not disturb the current working tree.

faq.question: Does it modify repository files?
faq.answer: It may add a worktree ignore rule to .gitignore when a project-local worktree directory is missing from ignore rules.

faq.question: Why does it run tests before implementation?
faq.answer: Baseline tests show whether the fresh worktree starts clean before new changes are introduced.

faq.question: Can it work outside the project directory?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports a global worktree location under the user's configuration directory when selected.

faq.question: What tools does it assume?
faq.answer: It assumes Git is available and may use common project tools such as npm, Cargo, pip, Poetry, pytest, or Go.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Setup and test commands can execute repository-controlled scripts, so review untrusted projects before running them.
